title: Design Thinking in HydraR
text: development HydraR guided need robust, reproducible, scalable framework agentic orchestration R, specifically scientific bioinformatics research. document outlines key design decisions trade-offs made development.

title: Design Thinking in HydraR
text: One primary design choices strict separation Orchestrator (AgentDAG) Executors (AgentDriver). Logic: DAG manages flow state decision-making graph. Execution: Drivers handle actual interaction external LLMs local tools. Trade-: adds complexity R6 class hierarchy ensures package provider-agnostic. can swap Claude-based driver Gemini-based one without changing workflow logic.

title: Design Thinking in HydraR
text: Unlike many frameworks prioritize latest REST API wrappers, HydraR prioritizes CLI interactions. ?: Scientific environments often limited egress require specific local environment discovery (like path resolution) CLIs handle better raw HTTP calls. CLIs like gemini-cli claude-code also manage authentication retry logic robustly. Trade-: requires user specific CLI tools installed, significantly reduces maintenance burden R package .

title: Design Thinking in HydraR
text: tasks involving file modifications (e.g., automated code fixing), HydraR uses Git Worktrees provide isolated execution environments. ?: Orchestrating multiple agents parallel directory leads race conditions inconsistent states. Worktrees allow agent (node) operate branch/copy repository without interfering others. Design Choice: chose Worktrees Docker containers keep framework “R-native” avoid overhead container management HPC environments.

title: Design Thinking in HydraR
text: HydraR uses AgentState backed high-performance SQL (DuckDB SQLite). Decision: Moving away complex nested R lists tabular/relational state model ensures checkpoints durable can audited run. Merit: enables “Time-Travel Debugging” researcher can inspect exact state workflow given node transition.

title: Design Thinking in HydraR
text: mermaid_parser.R integration designed make complex agentic graphs “human-readable” default. Philosophy: researcher visualize agent’s logic, trust . supporting round-trip Mermaid syntax, allow users design visual editor execute R, vice versa.

title: Agentic Academic Researcher
text: vignette demonstrates Academic Research pattern using HydraR. define linear, multi-stage agentic workflow designed synthesize academic literature final report using Gemini CLI. pipeline comprises three agents: 1. Searcher Node: Given research topic, finds relevant academic papers. 2. Summarizer Node: Ingests raw papers extracts key findings. 3. Compiler Node: Takes extracted findings formats comprehensive literature review.

title: Agentic Academic Researcher
text: HydraR supports optional state checkpointing node execution. Choose four modes: "none" – Ephemeral run, persistence (default). "memory" – -memory via MemorySaver (lost session exit). "rds" – File-based via RDSSaver (lightweight, base R ). "duckdb" – Database-backed via DuckDBSaver (requires duckdb package).
code: # ── USER CONFIGURATION ────────────────────────────────────────── CHECKPOINTER_MODE <- "none" # "none" | "memory" | "rds" | "duckdb" RDS_DIR <- "checkpoints" DUCKDB_PATH <- "checkpoints/academic_research.duckdb" THREAD_ID <- "academic_research_run_1" # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  checkpointer <- switch(CHECKPOINTER_MODE,   "memory" = MemorySaver$new(),   "rds" = RDSSaver$new(dir = RDS_DIR),   "duckdb" = {     if (!dir.exists(dirname(DUCKDB_PATH))) dir.create(dirname(DUCKDB_PATH), recursive = TRUE)     DuckDBSaver$new(db_path = DUCKDB_PATH)   },   "none" = NULL,   stop(sprintf("Invalid CHECKPOINTER_MODE: '%s'", CHECKPOINTER_MODE)) )  thread_id <- if (!is.null(checkpointer)) THREAD_ID else NULL

title: Agentic Blog Writer

Content creation often involves multi-stage pipeline consisting brainstorming, drafting, reviewing. model DAG mix linear execution cyclic feedback using Gemini CLI. Outliner Node: Generates structured outline based topic. Drafter Node: Takes outline writes draft. also reacts feedback Editor. title: Creating Custom Agent Drivers
text: HydraR built provider-agnostic architecture. ships drivers Gemini, Claude, OpenAI, can extend framework subclassing AgentDriver R6 class. guide covers: 1. Architecture: AgentDriver base class. 2. Mocking: Creating MockDriver testing. 3. CLI Pattern: Driving local tools. 4. API Pattern: Connecting cloud services. 5. Isolation: Integrating Git worktrees.

title: Creating Custom Agent Drivers
text: developing complex AgentDAG workflows, often want avoid cost latency real LLM calls. Mock Driver provides deterministic responses.

title: Creating Custom Agent Drivers
text: powerful mocking pattern Response Queuing, driver returns sequence pre-defined answers across multiple calls.
code: library(HydraR) library(R6)  MockDriver <- R6::R6Class("MockDriver",   inherit = AgentDriver,   public = list(     responses = list(),          initialize = function(id = "mock", responses = list(), ...) {       super$initialize(id = id, provider = "mock", ...)       self$responses <- responses     },          call = function(prompt, ...) {       if (length(self$responses) == 0) {         return("Default Mock Response")       }       # Pop the first response from the queue       res <- self$responses[[1]]       self$responses <- self$responses[-1]       return(res)     }   ) )  # Usage in a test driver <- MockDriver$new(responses = list("Answer A", "Answer B")) driver$call("Q1") # Returns "Answer A" driver$call("Q2") # Returns "Answer B"

title: Creating Custom Agent Drivers
text: CLI drivers primary way HydraR interacts tools like gemini-cli gh copilot.

title: Creating Custom Agent Drivers
text: Identify CLI: Determine command (e.g., -llm-cli). Define Supported Options: Populate self$supported_opts cli_opts validation. Use exec_in_dir: Always use built-helper respect worktree isolation.
code: CustomCLIDriver <- R6::R6Class("CustomCLIDriver",   inherit = AgentDriver,   public = list(     initialize = function(id = "custom_cli", ...) {       super$initialize(id = id, provider = "my-tool", ...)       # Define flags allowed in cli_opts (e.g., node$run(cli_opts = list(temperature = 0.7)))       self$supported_opts <- c("temperature", "top_p")     },          call = function(prompt, model = NULL, cli_opts = list(), ...) {       # 1. Format flags automatically (converts _ to -)       flags <- self$format_cli_opts(cli_opts)              # 2. Execute command       # exec_in_dir is critical for Git Worktree safety       res <- self$exec_in_dir(         command = "my-llm-cli",         args = c("generate", flags),         input = prompt,         stdout = TRUE       )              return(paste(res, collapse = "\n"))     }   ) )

title: Creating Custom Agent Drivers
text: cloud-based providers, use httr2 inside call() method.
code: CustomAPIDriver <- R6::R6Class("CustomAPIDriver",   inherit = AgentDriver,   public = list(     api_url = "https://api.example.com/v1/chat",          call = function(prompt, model = NULL, cli_opts = list(), ...) {       # Use httr2 for robust HTTP requests       req <- httr2::request(self$api_url) |>         httr2::req_body_json(list(prompt = prompt, config = cli_opts)) |>         httr2::req_perform()              resp <- httr2::resp_body_json(req)       return(resp$choices[[1]]$text)     }   ) )

title: Creating Custom Agent Drivers
text: unique feature HydraR ability run parallel file-modifying agents isolated Git Worktrees. driver developer, must ensure driver respects context.

title: Creating Custom Agent Drivers
text: AgentDAG runs use_worktrees = TRUE, node assigned isolated directory. path injected driver’s working_dir field CWD changed node’s execution context. [!IMPORTANT] CLI Drivers: Use self$exec_in_dir() internally uses withr::with_dir() ensure CLI sees worktree root. API Drivers: model needs read local files provide context, ensure look self$working_dir rather getwd().
code: # Internal logic in AgentDriver exec_in_dir = function(command, args, ...) {   if (!is.null(self$working_dir)) {     withr::with_dir(self$working_dir, {       system2(command, args, ...)     })   } else {     system2(command, args, ...)   } }

title: Creating Custom Agent Drivers
text: modern way integrate custom driver workflow Node Factory Mermaid Graph.
code: # 1. Define a factory that resolves your custom driver my_node_factory <- function(id, label, params) {   # Use parameters from the Mermaid graph to configure the driver   driver_obj <- if (params$driver == "custom") {     CustomCLIDriver$new(id = "my-bot")   } else {     NULL   }    AgentLLMNode$new(     id = id,     label = label,     driver = driver_obj,     role = "You are a specialized assistant.",     prompt_builder = function(state) sprintf("Process: %s", state$get("input"))   ) }  # 2. Define the workflow as a Mermaid string mermaid_graph <- " graph TD   A[Specialized Agent | driver=custom] "  # 3. Instantiate the DAG dag <- AgentDAG$from_mermaid(mermaid_graph, node_factory = my_node_factory)

title: Agentic Data Science Assistant
text: vignette demonstrates Data Science Assistant pattern using HydraR. machine learning workflows, hyperparameter tuning inherently iterative process. can map paradigm AgentDAG using Gemini CLI. workflow contains initial data setup phase followed cyclic model optimization phase: 1. Data Cleaner Node: Ingests prepares raw data analysis. 2. Model Trainer Node: Uses LLM recommend hyperparameters simulate model training. 3. Evaluator Node: Assesses model’s accuracy. falls target threshold, generates specific refinement feedback loops back re-optimization.

title: Agentic Data Science Assistant
text: keep architecture clean, store workflow components—initial configuration, LLM prompts, agent roles, deterministic logic—central registry.
code: ds_logic_registry <- list(   # 0. Initial Configuration   initial_state = list(     raw_data = "Titanic_Dataset.csv",     target_accuracy = 0.85,     total_evaluations = 0   ),    # 1. Deterministic Logic Functions   logic = list(     DataCleaner = function(state, params) {       dataset <- state$get("raw_data")       clean_data <- paste("Cleaned", dataset)       list(status = "SUCCESS", output = list(clean_data = clean_data))     },     Evaluator = function(state, params) {       config <- state$get("ModelTrainer")       target <- state$get("target_accuracy")       iteration <- state$get("total_evaluations") + 1        accuracy <- min(0.60 + (iteration * 0.10), 0.95)        if (accuracy >= target) {         list(status = "SUCCESS", output = list(           optimization_complete = TRUE,           eval_message = sprintf("Target reached: %.2f >= %.2f using config: %s", accuracy, target, config),           total_evaluations = iteration         ))       } else {         list(status = "SUCCESS", output = list(           optimization_complete = FALSE,           eval_message = sprintf("Accuracy %.2f is below %.2f. Recommending new parameters.", accuracy, target),           total_evaluations = iteration         ))       }     }   ),    # 2. LLM Agent Roles   roles = list(     ModelTrainer = "You are an AutoML expert. Given a dataset and previous accuracy logs, recommend a new set of hyperparameters."   ),    # 3. LLM Prompt Builders   prompts = list(     ModelTrainer = function(state) {       feedback_text <- if (!is.null(state$get("Evaluator"))) sprintf("\nFeedback: %s", state$get("Evaluator")) else ""       sprintf("Dataset: %s%s\nOutput exactly a model configuration string.", state$get("clean_data"), feedback_text)     }   ) )

title: Agentic Data Science Assistant
text: use factory function dynamically create nodes based type parameters defined Mermaid graph.
code: ds_node_factory <- function(id, label, params) {   # Driver resolution from Mermaid params   driver_obj <- if (!is.null(params$driver) && params$driver == "gemini") GeminiCLIDriver$new() else NULL    if (id %in% names(ds_logic_registry$logic)) {     # Create a deterministic Logic Node     AgentLogicNode$new(       id = id,       label = label,       logic_fn = ds_logic_registry$logic[[id]]     )   } else {     # Create an agentic LLM Node     AgentLLMNode$new(       id = id,       label = label,       role = ds_logic_registry$roles[[id]],       driver = driver_obj,       prompt_builder = ds_logic_registry$prompts[[id]]     )   } }

title: Distributed Consensus Simulation
text: Communication HydraR handled RestrictedState wrapper, provides node private inbox. ensures nodes can see messages explicitly sent .
code: graph TD   V1[Voter Alpha] -- vote --> L[Leader]   V2[Voter Beta] -- vote --> L   V3[Voter Gamma] -- vote --> L

title: Distributed Consensus Simulation
text: First, load HydraR package initialize MemoryMessageLog audit simulation.
code: # install.packages("devtools") # Run if devtools is not installed devtools::install_github("APAF-bioinformatics/HydraR")  # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("APAF-bioinformatics/HydraR")  devtools::load_all() # Quickly loads all local changes into your session library(HydraR)  # Initialize a persistent, parallel-safe audit log log_path <- file.path(tempdir(), "consensus_msgs.jsonl") message_log <- JSONLMessageLog$new(path = log_path)

title: Distributed Consensus Simulation
text: keep architecture clean, store deterministic logic Voters Leader central registry.
code: consensus_logic_registry <- list(   # 1. Deterministic Logic Functions   logic = list(     Voter = function(state, params = NULL) {       # 1. Decide randomly       vote <- sample(c("SUCCESS", "FAILURE"), 1)        # 2. Send private message to the Leader       state$send_message(to = "Leader", content = list(vote = vote))        message(sprintf("   [%s] Voted: %s", state$node_id, vote))       list(status = "SUCCESS", output = vote)     },     Leader = function(state, params = NULL) {       # 1. Retrieve messages from private inbox       msgs <- state$receive_messages()        if (length(msgs) == 0) {         return(list(status = "FAILED", output = "nothing to tabulate"))       }        # 2. Extract and count votes       votes <- sapply(msgs, function(m) m$content$vote)       counts <- table(votes)        # 3. Determine majority       majority <- names(counts)[which.max(counts)]        message(sprintf("   [Leader] Received %d votes. Consensus: %s", length(votes), majority))       list(status = "SUCCESS", output = list(final_consensus = majority, vote_table = as.list(counts)))     }   ) )

title: Isolated Execution with Git Worktrees
text: major challenge building autonomous agents isolation. agent modifies file (e.g., writing report fixing bug), can easily cause conflicts parallel agents user’s main working directory. HydraR solves natively integrating Git Worktrees. guide demonstrates “Toy Program” agent creates new file isolated worktree.

what: 🏗️ Step 1: Initialize a Temporary Git Repository
title: Isolated Execution with Git Worktrees
text: avoid modifying actual project, ’ll create temporary Git repository tempdir().
code: library(HydraR) library(withr)  # 1. Create a temporary folder repo_root <- file.path(tempdir(), "hydra-toy-repo") dir.create(repo_root)  # 2. Initialize Git and a README withr::with_dir(repo_root, {   system("git init -b main")   system("git config user.email 'apaf@example.com'")   system("git config user.name 'APAF Agent'")   writeLines("# Toy Project", "README.md")   system("git add README.md")   system("git commit -m 'Initial commit'") })

title: Isolated Execution with Git Worktrees
text: Conflicts: Two parallel agents can modify DESCRIPTION time without corrupted git states. Explicit Merging: Changes appear main directory MergeHarmonizer node explicitly merges DAG succeeds. title: Agentic Travel Planning: Sydney to Hong Kong
text: workflow demonstrates HydraR enables Truly Zero-R-Code definitions: 1. Language-Independent: workflow defined YAML Mermaid, making portable. 2. Reduced Boilerplate: load_workflow() handles registration state parsing. 3. Maintainable: Logic roles separated R execution engine.

title: HydraR: The Complete Instruction Manual
text: HydraR professional-grade agentic orchestration framework built R. enables design, execution, monitoring complex multi-agent workflows using Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) architecture supports iterative loops, parallel execution, persistent state. Developed APAF Bioinformatics, HydraR addresses need durable, CLI-first agentic systems can operate reliably scientific software engineering environments.

title: HydraR: The Complete Instruction Manual
text: framework built around four primary R6 classes: AgentDAG: central orchestrator manages nodes, edges, execution flow. Usually instantiated via AgentDAG$from_mermaid(). AgentLLMNode: Interfaces Large Language Models via Drivers. AgentLogicNode: Executes deterministic R functions Logic Registry. AgentState: structured, centrally managed container data shared nodes. AgentDriver: provider-agnostic interface communicating LLMs (e.g., GeminiCLIDriver).

title: HydraR: The Complete Instruction Manual
text: recommended way build DAG HydraR using Mermaid--Source pattern. involves defining visual graph “registry” logic.
code: library(HydraR)  # 1. Define a logic registry logic_registry <- list(   logic = list(     Hello = function(state, params = NULL) {       name <- state$get("user_name", "Stranger")       list(status = "SUCCESS", output = list(greeting = paste("Hello,", name)))     }   ) )  # 2. Define a node factory node_factory <- function(id, label, params) {   AgentLogicNode$new(id = id, label = label, logic_fn = logic_registry$logic[[id]]) }  # 3. Build from Mermaid and run mermaid_graph <- "graph TD; Hello[Greet User]" dag <- AgentDAG$from_mermaid(mermaid_graph, node_factory = node_factory)  result <- dag$compile()$run(initial_state = list(user_name = "Hydra User")) print(result$state$get("greeting")) #> [1] "Hello, Hydra User"

title: HydraR: The Complete Instruction Manual
text: HydraR supports conditional edges defined Mermaid graph added programmatically. enables complex “self-healing” behaviors.
code: # In your Mermaid graph: # Reviewer -- Needs Revision --> Writer  # Add the logic for the transition dag$add_conditional_edge(   from = "Reviewer",   test = function(out) isTRUE(out$valid),   if_true = NULL,         # End execution   if_false = "Writer"     # Loop back )

title: HydraR: The Complete Instruction Manual
text: integrating furrr package Git Worktrees, HydraR can execute independent branches DAG parallel isolated environments.
code: # Run the DAG with worktree isolation enabled result <- dag$run(   initial_state = init,   use_worktrees = TRUE,   repo_root = getwd() # Current repository )

title: HydraR: The Complete Instruction Manual
text: AgentState ensures agents consistent view world. can use Reducers control state updated multiple nodes modify variable.
code: state <- AgentState$new(   initial_data = list(logs = list()),   reducers = list(logs = reducer_append) )  # Any node updating 'logs' will now append to the existing list  # instead of overwriting it.

title: HydraR: The Complete Instruction Manual
text: long-running workflows, HydraR provides Checkpointer system. execution interrupted, can resumed last successful node using unique thread_id. Supported backends: - MemorySaver: -memory storage (default). - RDSSaver: File-based storage. - DuckDBSaver: High-performance persistent database.
code: saver <- DuckDBSaver$new(db_path = "history.duckdb") result <- dag$run(   initial_state = init,   checkpointer = saver,   thread_id = "research-session-001" )

title: HydraR: The Complete Instruction Manual
text: HydraR driver-agnostic. Use Node Factory resolve drivers based parameters embedded Mermaid labels.
code: # Mermaid: Consultant[Expert | driver=gemini]  node_factory <- function(id, label, params) {   driver_obj <- if (isTRUE(params$driver == "gemini")) GeminiCLIDriver$new() else NULL      AgentLLMNode$new(     id = id,     label = label,     driver = driver_obj,     role = "Expert Consultant",     prompt_builder = function(state) sprintf("Analyze: %s", state$get("problem"))   ) }

title: Advanced Mermaid Parameters
text: keep architecture clean, store logic custom nodes central registry.
code: param_logic_registry <- list(   # 1. Custom Node Logic   logic = list(     Default = function(state, params = list()) {       # This function will be called by our CustomNode class       param_str <- if (length(params) > 0) {         paste(names(params), params, sep = "=", collapse = ", ")       } else {         "none"       }       message(sprintf("   [%s] Executing logic... (Params: %s)", state$node_id, param_str))       list(status = "SUCCESS", output = paste("Result from", state$node_id))     }   ) )

title: Advanced Mermaid Parameters
text: use factory function dynamically resolve nodes inject parameters parsed Mermaid graph.
code: # 1. Define a Specialized Node Factory param_node_factory <- function(id, label, params = list()) {   # Create a custom node class for this example   CustomNode <- R6::R6Class("CustomNode",     inherit = AgentNode,     public = list(       run = function(state) {         # Delegate to our registry logic         param_logic_registry$logic$Default(state, self$params)       }     )   )    CustomNode$new(id, label, params) }

title: Agentic Personalized Shopping Assistant
text: vignette demonstrates Personalized Shopping Assistant pattern using HydraR Gemini CLI. define agentic graph recommendation engine interacts mock user. two roles : 1. Shopper Node: Acts intelligent proxy searches catalogue based preferences. 2. User Proxy Node: Represents user feedback loop. provides critiques recommended item. models continuous interaction loop agent refines search based real-time feedback.

title: Agentic Personalized Shopping Assistant
text: keep architecture clean, store workflow components—initial configuration, LLM prompts, agent roles—central registry.
code:
shopping_logic_registry <- list(   # 0. Initial Shopping Request   initial_state = list(     shopping_request = "I need a cool t-shirt for the weekend with graphic design."   ),    # 1. Agent Roles   roles = list(     Shopper = "You are a personalized shopping assistant. Based on the user's initial request and any feedback provide a specific product recommendation.",     UserProxy = "You are a customer looking for a specific item. Review the recommended product and either say 'I'll buy it' or provide 'Modification Feedback'."   ),    # 2. Prompt Builders   prompts = list(     Shopper = function(state) {       feedback_text <- if (!is.null(state$get("UserProxy"))) sprintf("
User Feedback: %s", state$get("UserProxy")) else ""       sprintf("Original Request: %s%s
Output exactly a product name.", state$get("shopping_request"), feedback_text)     },     UserProxy = function(state) {       sprintf("Recommended Item: %s
Original Request: %s
Decide if you like it or need something different.", state$get("Shopper"), state$get("shopping_request"))     }   ) )

title: Agentic Personalized Shopping Assistant
text: use factory function dynamically create nodes based parameters defined Mermaid graph.
code: shopping_node_factory <- function(id, label, params) {   # Driver resolution from Mermaid params   driver_obj <- if (params$driver == "gemini") GeminiCLIDriver$new() else NULL    AgentLLMNode$new(     id = id,     label = label,     role = shopping_logic_registry$roles[[id]],     driver = driver_obj,     prompt_builder = shopping_logic_registry$prompts[[id]]   ) }

title: Mermaid Round-Trip Visualization
text: keep architecture clean, store deterministic logic functions central registry.
code: round_trip_logic_registry <- list(   # 1. Deterministic Logic Functions   logic = list(     Check = function(state, params = NULL) {       # A logic node that fails the first time but succeeds the second       run_count <- state$get("check_runs") %||% 0       state$set("check_runs", run_count + 1)        if (run_count == 0) {         cat("Quality check failed. Routing to ReSearch...\n")         return(list(status = "SUCCESS", output = FALSE))       } else {         cat("Quality check passed!\n")         return(list(status = "SUCCESS", output = TRUE))       }     },     Default = function(state, params = NULL) {       list(status = "SUCCESS", output = paste("Result from", state$node_id))     }   ) )

code: round_trip_node_factory <- function(id, label, params) {   logic_fn <- round_trip_logic_registry$logic[[id]] %||% round_trip_logic_registry$logic$Default    AgentLogicNode$new(     id = id,     label = label,     logic_fn = logic_fn   ) }

title: Agentic Software Bug Assistant
text: vignette demonstrates Software Bug Assistant pattern using HydraR Gemini CLI. scenario, two agent nodes work together resolve software bug: 1. Bug Analyzer Node: Reviews error message current code, proposes patch. 2. Test Runner Node: Simulates running test suite proposed patch. test fails, provides error traces back Bug Analyzer. classic “Generate--Test” self-healing loop.

title: Agentic Software Bug Assistant
text: keep architecture clean, store workflow components—initial configuration, LLM prompts, agent roles, deterministic logic—central registry.
code: bug_logic_registry <- list(   # 0. Initial Configuration   initial_state = list(     bug_report = "Function crashes when 'x' is missing."   ),    # 1. Deterministic Logic Functions   logic = list(     Tester = function(state, params) {       patch <- state$get("Analyzer")        # Mock evaluation logic: In our scenario, the fix must use is.null().       if (grepl("is.null", patch, fixed = TRUE)) {         list(status = "SUCCESS", output = list(           tests_passed = TRUE,           test_feedback = "All 5 tests passed successfully."         ))       } else {         list(status = "SUCCESS", output = list(           tests_passed = FALSE,           test_feedback = "Error: object 'NULL' not found. Did you mean to use 'is.null()'?"         ))       }     }   ),    # 2. LLM Agent Roles   roles = list(     Analyzer = "You are a software engineer specializing in fixing R bugs. Review the bug report and any previous test failures, then provide a corrected R code snippet."   ),    # 3. LLM Prompt Builders   prompts = list(     Analyzer = function(state) {       feedback_text <- if (!is.null(state$get("Tester"))) sprintf("\nTest Feedback: %s", state$get("Tester")) else ""       sprintf("Bug Report: %s%s\nOutput exactly a snippet of R code.", state$get("bug_report"), feedback_text)     }   ) )

title: Agentic Software Bug Assistant
text: use factory function dynamically create nodes based type parameters defined Mermaid graph.
code: bug_node_factory <- function(id, label, params) {   # Driver resolution from Mermaid params   driver_obj <- if (!is.null(params$driver) && params$driver == "gemini") GeminiCLIDriver$new() else NULL    if (id %in% names(bug_logic_registry$logic)) {     # Create a deterministic Logic Node     AgentLogicNode$new(       id = id,       label = label,       logic_fn = bug_logic_registry$logic[[id]]     )   } else {     # Create an agentic LLM Node     AgentLLMNode$new(       id = id,       label = label,       role = bug_logic_registry$roles[[id]],       driver = driver_obj,       prompt_builder = bug_logic_registry$prompts[[id]]     )   } }

Ensure conditional edges have exit conditions. #> Graph compiled successfully.

title: Parallel Benchmarking with Git Worktrees
text: Modern agentic workflows often involve multiple specialized agents working different parts codebase simultaneously. agents modify files, risk stepping ’s toes—causing merge conflicts corrupted git states. HydraR addresses leveraging Git Worktrees isolated execution. guide demonstrates parallel workflow : 1. Three Agents simultaneously write R code different sorting algorithms (Bubble, Quick, Merge sort) separate worktrees. 2. Merge Harmonizer automatically merges independent branches back main repository. 3. Benchmarking Node executes generated code compare performance. 4. Visualization Node plots results.

title: Parallel Benchmarking with Git Worktrees
text: demonstrate power worktrees, first create temporary Git repository.
code: library(HydraR) library(withr) library(ggplot2) library(future) library(furrr)  # Check for Anthropic API Key (Needed for refined agents) # Must be done BEFORE future::plan so workers inherit the environment if (Sys.getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY") == "") {   if (file.exists("../.Renviron")) readRenviron("../.Renviron")   if (file.exists(".Renviron")) readRenviron(".Renviron") }  if (Sys.getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY") == "") {   stop("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY not found. Please set it in your .Renviron or workspace.") }  # Ensure Gemini CLI path is configured (Environment variables are inherited by workers) Sys.setenv(HYDRAR_GEMINI_PATH = "/opt/homebrew/bin/gemini")  # 0. Setup Parallel Execution (for Worktree Isolation) future::plan(future::multisession, workers = 3) options(future.rng.onMisuse = "ignore")  # 1. Create a temporary folder repo_root <- file.path(tempdir(), "sorting-benchmark-repo") if (dir.exists(repo_root)) unlink(repo_root, recursive = TRUE) dir.create(repo_root)  # 2. Initialize Git and a README withr::with_dir(repo_root, {   system2("git", c("init"))   system2("git", c("config", "user.email", "apaf@example.com"))   system2("git", c("config", "user.name", "APAF Agent"))   system2("git", c("config", "commit.gpgsign", "false"))   writeLines("# Sorting Benchmark", "README.md")   system2("git", c("add", "README.md"))   system2("git", c("commit", "-m", "Initial_commit"))   system2("git", c("branch", "-M", "main")) })

title: Parallel Benchmarking with Git Worktrees
text: Rather registering roles logic manually R, now define entire workflow architecture (graph, prompts, logic) single declarative YAML file. follows APAF-Agentic-Standard Zero-R-Code definitions.
code: # 1. Load the workflow manifest wf <- load_workflow("sorting_benchmark.yml")  # 2. Extract components mermaid_graph <- wf$graph initial_state <- wf$initial_state  # 3. Inject dynamic environment variables into initial state initial_state$repo_root <- repo_root initial_state$output_dir <- file.path(getwd(), "..", "paper", "figures")  # 4. Instantiate and Compile dag <- AgentDAG$from_mermaid(mermaid_graph, node_factory = auto_node_factory()) compiled_dag <- dag$compile()  # 5. Run with Worktree Isolation # Ensure workers inherit our environment (for API keys and PATH) future::plan(future::multisession, workers = 3)  results <- compiled_dag$run(   initial_state = initial_state,   use_worktrees = TRUE,   repo_root = repo_root,   fail_if_dirty = FALSE,   packages = c("withr", "HydraR", "ggplot2") )  # 6. Save Execution Trace compiled_dag$save_trace("sorting_trace.json")

title: State Persistence & Restart: Resilient Workflows
text: run DAG fixed = FALSE. second node pause pipeline save state DuckDB.
code: # Configure DuckDB Persistence saver <- DuckDBSaver$new(db_path = "history.duckdb") tid <- "session-001"  # Run 1: Expected to pause at Step2 res1 <- dag$run(   thread_id = tid,   checkpointer = saver,   initial_state = list(fixed = FALSE) ) #> Graph compiled successfully. #> [2026-04-05 10:15:53] [Iteration] Restored state from checkpoint for thread: session-001 #> [2026-04-05 10:15:53] [Linear] Running Node: Step1 #>    [Step1] Executing R logic... #> [2026-04-05 10:15:53] [Linear] Running Node: Step2 #>    [Step2] Executing R logic... #> [2026-04-05 10:15:53] [Linear] Running Node: Step3 #>    [Step3] Executing R logic...  print(res1$status) # "PAUSE" #> [1] "completed"

title: State Persistence & Restart: Resilient Workflows
text: Next, “fix” state setting fixed = TRUE restart Step2. HydraR detects checkpoint, restores state DuckDB, re-executes paused node.
code: # Run 2: Resume from Step2 using the SAME thread_id final_results <- dag$run(   thread_id = tid,   checkpointer = saver,   initial_state = list(fixed = TRUE),   resume_from = "Step2" ) #> Graph compiled successfully. #> [2026-04-05 10:15:53] [Iteration] Restored state from checkpoint for thread: session-001 #> [2026-04-05 10:15:53] [Resuming] Linear DAG Execution from node: Step2 #> [2026-04-05 10:15:53] [Linear] Running Node: Step2 #>    [Step2] Executing R logic... #> [2026-04-05 10:15:53] [Linear] Running Node: Step3 #>    [Step3] Executing R logic...  # Step 1 was skipped; the process started from Step 2! print(final_results$status) # "SUCCESS" #> [1] "completed"

title: Agentic Story Teller
text: vignette demonstrates Story Teller pattern using HydraR Gemini CLI. workflow, two LLM agents collaborate write story: 1. Writer Agent: Drafts story based prompt incorporates feedback. 2. Reviewer Agent: Critiques draft using rubric. story meets criteria, approves draft. Otherwise, sends feedback back Writer. forms fundamental iterative critique--revise loop.

title: Agentic Story Teller
text: keep architecture clean, store workflow components—initial configuration, LLM prompts, agent roles—central registry.
code:
story_logic_registry <- list(   # 0. Initial Story Prompt   initial_state = list(     story_prompt = "A story about a robot learning to cook."   ),    # 1. Agent Roles   roles = list(     Writer = "You are a creative writer. Draft a story based on the initial prompt or update it according to reviewer feedback. Output exactly the story draft.",     Reviewer = "You are a literary editor. Critically review the story draft for tone and quality. If it is excellent, say 'Approved'. Otherwise, give specific critique."   ),    # 2. Prompt Builders   prompts = list(     Writer = function(state) {       feedback_text <- if (!is.null(state$get("Reviewer"))) sprintf("
Feedback: %s", state$get("Reviewer")) else ""       sprintf("Prompt: %s%s
Draft the story.", state$get("story_prompt"), feedback_text)     },     Reviewer = function(state) {       sprintf("Draft: %s
Review this draft.", state$get("Writer"))     }   ) )

title: Agentic Story Teller
text: use factory function dynamically create nodes based parameters defined Mermaid graph.
code: story_node_factory <- function(id, label, params) {   # Driver resolution from Mermaid params   driver_obj <- if (params$driver == "gemini") GeminiCLIDriver$new() else NULL    AgentLLMNode$new(     id = id,     label = label,     role = story_logic_registry$roles[[id]],     driver = driver_obj,     prompt_builder = story_logic_registry$prompts[[id]]   ) }

title: Agentic Story Teller
text: define entire workflow architecture Mermaid string. string serves single source truth structure node metadata.

title: Agentic Travel Booking Concierge
text: vignette demonstrates orchestrate Travel Booking Concierge workflow using HydraR. typical travel planning AI must satisfy various constraints user’s destination, duration, crucially, budget. workflow, define two nodes: 1. Travel Planner Node: Generates proposed itinerary estimated cost based user constraints previous feedback. 2. Itinerary Validator Node: Receives proposed itinerary, checks user’s hard budget constraints, either approves demands cheaper alternative. demonstrates robust condition handling cyclic loops.

title: Agentic Travel Booking Concierge
text: node mocks LLM calling flight hotel APIs, receiving feedback previous proposal expensive.
code: planner_node <- AgentLogicNode$new(id = "Planner", logic_fn = function(state, memory = NULL) {   # Read user constraints   destination <- state$get("destination")   budget <- state$get("budget")    # Check if we are iterating due to failure   attempts <- state$get("planner_attempts")   if (is.null(attempts)) attempts <- 0   attempts <- attempts + 1    # Mocking cost reduction on each attempt   # Attempt 1: $2500, Attempt 2: $1800, Attempt 3: $1200   base_cost <- 3200   proposed_cost <- base_cost - (attempts * 700)    itinerary <- sprintf("Trip to %s. Estimated Cost: $%d.", destination, proposed_cost)    list(     status = "SUCCESS",     output = list(       proposed_itinerary = itinerary,       proposed_cost = proposed_cost,       planner_attempts = attempts     )   ) })  dag$add_node(planner_node)

title: Agentic Travel Booking Concierge
text: node ensures LLM’s proposal strictly adheres numerical budget limit.
code: validator_node <- AgentLogicNode$new(id = "Validator", logic_fn = function(state, memory = NULL) {   proposed_cost <- state$get("proposed_cost")   budget <- state$get("budget")    if (proposed_cost <= budget) {     list(       status = "SUCCESS",       output = list(         is_valid = TRUE,         validation_message = "Itinerary meets budget requirements. Booking confirmed."       )     )   } else {     list(       status = "SUCCESS",       output = list(         is_valid = FALSE,         validation_message = sprintf("Proposed cost ($%d) exceeds budget ($%d). Find cheaper options.", proposed_cost, budget)       )     )   } })  dag$add_node(validator_node)

title: Agentic Travel Booking Concierge
text: define looping logic. Validator sends control back Planner is_valid FALSE.
code: dag$set_start_node("Planner")  dag$add_edge("Planner", "Validator")  dag$add_conditional_edge(   from = "Validator",   test = function(out) {     isTRUE(out$is_valid)   },   if_true = NULL, # Ends execution successfully   if_false = "Planner" # Loop back for a cheaper proposal )  compiled_dag <- dag$compile() #> Graph compiled successfully.

title: HydraR 0.2.0-alpha (Standardized Drivers)
text: Standardized Driver Architecture: Renamed core AI drivers internal consistency Provider + Mode clarity (e.g., AnthropicCLIDriver, AnthropicAPIDriver, OpenAIAPIDriver). OpenAI Shorthand Refactor: Swapped driver=openai favor high-performance Codex CLI default, driver=openai_api cloud reasoning. Anthropic Shorthand Update: Renamed driver=claude driver=anthropic match provider-first naming standards. Improved GPT-5.4 Support: Updated OpenAIAPIDriver defaults seamless integration GPT-5.4 flagship/mini models. Flagship Orchestration Vignette: Introduced Advanced Orchestration 2026 flagship documentation. R6 Standards: Resolved internal finalize() warnings ensure full compliance R6 2.4.0+ suppressed developmental noise.

title: HydraR 0.1.0 (Initial Release)
text: Agentic Orchestration: Implementation AgentDAG R6 class managing complex directed workflows iterative loops (state machines). Hardened State Management: Introduced AgentState system, transitioning nested R lists robust key-value store support complex reducers history tracking. Provider-Agnostic Drivers: Initial support GeminiCLIDriver, AnthropicCLIDriver, CopilotCLIDriver, enabling “hot-swappable” LLM backends. Git Worktree Isolation: Developed worktree.R module provide safe, parallel execution environments file-modifying agentic workflows. High-Fidelity Validation: Integrated Auditor Harmonizer patterns autonomous quality control merging distributed agent outputs. Persistence & Checkpointing: DuckDB SQLite-backed AgentCheckpointer implemented resumable auditable execution. Mermaid Round-Trip: Visual design engine converting R objects Mermaid.js syntax, enabling human---loop auditability. AI Transparency: Disclosure LLM usage development agents.md DESIGN.md.
